name: Architecture Judgment
definition: Evaluating system design tradeoffs with enough depth to challenge, validate, or redirect engineering proposals
professionalApplication: Knowing when to push back on a proposed runtime, sandbox, or deployment architecture with specific reasoning about cost, latency, isolation strength, and compatibility.

levels.0: Understand the primitives of modern web execution — browsers, frameworks, build systems, containers, CDNs. Build the vocabulary required to follow engineering conversations.
levels.1: Compare product architectures across the AI code-generation landscape. Recognize why v0, Lovable, Bolt, and Replit Agent made different architectural bets and what each optimizes for.
levels.2: Evaluate runtime and sandbox options with specific tradeoffs. Argue for WebContainers over Docker (or the reverse) based on user profile, cost structure, and isolation requirements.
levels.3: Choose preview and deployment infrastructure. Design tenant isolation, hot reload strategies, and rollback mechanisms that match the product's latency and reliability targets.
levels.4: Assess coding agent architecture. Decide between building agent infrastructure in-house, integrating Claude Code, or forking an open-source framework — and defend the decision with concrete tradeoffs.
levels.5: Design eval pipeline architecture. Separate functional correctness, visual fidelity, and qualitative quality into distinct measurement layers with appropriate methodologies for each.
levels.6: Instrument the platform with observability that matches its failure modes. Design monitoring for silent degradation, not just hard outages.
levels.7: Own full-stack architectural vision across runtime, preview, deploy, agent, eval, and extensibility. Sequence investments across multiple quarters without creating dependency traps.

name: Abstraction Design
definition: Defining the right boundaries between platform and product — what to expose, what to hide, what to make configurable
professionalApplication: Writing API surfaces, plugin specifications, and configuration models that serve both technical users who want control and non-technical users who want abstraction.

levels.0: Recognize where abstraction boundaries live in web platforms — HTML/CSS/JS, framework layer, build layer, deployment layer.
levels.1: Identify what belongs at the platform layer versus the product layer. Distinguish generic infrastructure concerns from product-specific ones.
levels.2: Define execution API boundaries. Decide what the runtime exposes to generated code versus what it isolates.
levels.3: Design tenant isolation boundaries and deployment API surfaces. Make promises about what users can and cannot customize.
levels.4: Design tool-use contracts for agents. Define what tools the agent can call, with what parameters, under what permission model.
levels.5: Separate eval concerns into distinct, loosely-coupled layers — functional correctness, quality, regression, safety — each evaluable independently.
levels.6: Design observability APIs that serve multiple audiences — engineering on-call, product analytics, leadership dashboards.
levels.7: Govern the platform-product boundary across multiple consuming teams. Design extensibility APIs that preserve platform invariants while enabling third-party innovation.

name: Failure Reasoning
definition: Thinking in failure modes — what breaks, who notices, how bad is it, what is the blast radius
professionalApplication: Before approving any design, enumerating the ways it can fail — silent failures, cascading failures, partial failures — and the observability needed to detect each.

levels.0: Recognize common web platform failure patterns — network errors, browser quirks, build failures, cold starts.
levels.1: Identify failure patterns specific to AI code generation — hallucinations, prompt-output drift, state corruption.
levels.2: Reason about runtime-specific failures — OOM kills, resource exhaustion, sandbox escapes, cold start cascades.
levels.3: Anticipate preview environment failure modes — tenant interference, HMR breaks, dependency install timeouts, deploy failures.
levels.4: Anticipate agent failure modes — multi-step reasoning failures, state corruption, tool call failures, context overflow.
levels.5: Anticipate eval system failures — false positives, false negatives, eval gaming, coverage gaps, judge miscalibration.
levels.6: Design incident response for AI-specific failures. Recognize that gradual degradation is more dangerous than acute outages.
levels.7: Reason about cascading failures across the platform. Design blast-radius limits and graceful degradation at every boundary.

name: Systems Sequencing
definition: Deciding what to build first when everything depends on everything else, and articulating why
professionalApplication: Writing multi-quarter roadmaps where dependencies, competitive pressure, and reliability targets all compete for the same engineering capacity — and defending the sequence with data.

levels.0: Understand how web platform primitives build on each other — you need a runtime before you need a build system before you need a deployment platform.
levels.1: Sequence market entry decisions. Recognize why competitors made certain architectural choices in certain orders.
levels.2: Sequence runtime investments ahead of the surfaces that depend on them.
levels.3: Sequence preview infrastructure, deployment pipeline, and observability in the correct dependency order.
levels.4: Sequence agent infrastructure, eval system, and production release gates so each is validated before being depended upon.
levels.5: Sequence eval investment before scale, not after. Understand the compounding cost of quality regressions that ship before detection infrastructure exists.
levels.6: Sequence observability investments to catch the failure modes that matter most at the current scale — not all failure modes deserve equal instrumentation upfront.
levels.7: Own multi-quarter platform roadmapping. Balance runtime rewrites, feature velocity, GA readiness, and extensibility APIs across a 12-18 month horizon.
